s: 1.5 million buried here — and the watchtowers were built to stop bodysnatchers
w: Daniel O'Connell founded this 124-acre cemetery in 1832 — the first place Catholics could bury their dead with dignity after the Penal Laws. The high walls and watchtowers were built specifically to deter bodysnatchers. Parnell, Collins, de Valera, Markievicz: the whole arc of nationalist Ireland.
_facts: Founded 1832 by Daniel O'Connell, first non-denominational public cemetery in Ireland
124 acres, over 1.5 million interred including Collins, de Valera, Parnell, Markievicz
High walls and watchtowers built to deter bodysnatchers active in Dublin in the early 19th century
Catholics previously barred from performing public funeral rites under the Penal Laws

s: Where the 1916 leaders were shot — and the youngest prisoner was three years old
w: Open 1796, closed 1924, nearly demolished in 1936. Pearse, Connolly, and twelve other leaders of the Easter Rising were executed in its stonebreakers' yard. The youngest recorded prisoner was three, arrested for petty theft. Volunteers restored it by hand in the 1960s — the state had shown no interest.
_facts: Built 1796, decommissioned 1924
Irish government considered demolition in 1936; cost deemed prohibitive
1916 Easter Rising leaders including Pearse and Connolly executed in the prison yard
Youngest prisoner reportedly three years old, arrested for petty theft
Volunteers restored the gaol in the 1960s before it opened as a museum

s: Ireland's first public library — the bookcases still have Easter Rising bullet holes
w: Built in 1707 for Archbishop Narcissus Marsh, Ireland's first public library still operates in its original form. The oak bookcases have bullet holes from the 1916 fighting at Jacob's Biscuit Factory next door. Three iron 'cages' were installed in the 1770s; readers were locked inside with the rare books they requested.
_facts: Opened 1707, Ireland's first public library, still used for its original purpose
Designed by Sir William Robinson; established by act of the Irish Parliament
Contains 25,000 books from the 16th-18th centuries, 300 manuscripts, 80 incunabula
Bookcases have bullet holes from 1916 Easter Rising at adjacent Jacob's Biscuit Factory
Three iron 'cages' installed 1770s — readers locked inside with rare books

s: A coaching inn since 1754, on a site licensed for ale since 1661
w: Built as a coaching inn in 1754 on a site in use since at least 1613, with an ale licence from 1661. Wolfe Tone and Robert Emmet are said to have drunk here; the 1798 rebels reputedly plotted in its rooms. The low-ceilinged courtyard is essentially unchanged since the 18th century.
_facts: Coaching inn built 1754 on a site known from at least 1613
Ale licence granted 1661
Wolfe Tone and Robert Emmet among figures said to have frequented it
1798 rebels reputedly plotted in its rooms
Original 18th-century courtyard survives

s: The pharmacy where Bloom bought lemon soap — still selling it, run by volunteers
w: Open since 1847, now run by volunteers. In Ulysses, Leopold Bloom buys a bar of lemon soap here on 16 June 1904. The shop still sells the same soap. On Bloomsday the queue goes round the block; on ordinary days, readings begin whenever someone turns up.
_facts: Operating since 1847; now run entirely by volunteers
Featured in James Joyce's Ulysses (1922), 'Lestrygonians' chapter
Leopold Bloom buys lemon soap here in the novel, set 16 June 1904
Still sells the lemon soap; daily Joyce readings held on the premises
Major pilgrimage point on Bloomsday (16 June)

s: An 1895 Victorian pub hidden in a Dame Court alley — Educating Rita was filmed here
w: Records of a pub here go to 1770; the current Victorian interior dates from 1895. Mahogany bar, cut glass, a tiled mosaic advertising it on Dame Street — some distance from the actual entrance. The clock still reads 'Tyson', after the original publican. Educating Rita and A Man of No Importance both filmed inside.
_facts: Records of a pub on this site from 1770; rebuilt in 1895
Victorian interior: mahogany bar, cut glass, ornate tiling
Tiled advertising mosaic on Dame Street (separate from the pub entrance at Dame Court)
Filmed in Educating Rita (1983) and A Man of No Importance (1994)
Clock and ironwork still carry 'Tyson', after the original builder

s: Since 1820, Dublin's journalists' pub — Judy Garland drank here after her shows
w: A pub at this Poolbeg Street address since 1820; Joyce name-drops it in 'Counterparts'. The Theatre Royal stood across the road — its showbills still hang here, beside an autographed photo of Judy Garland, who performed there and came in after. One of the least-adorned, most argued-about pints in the city.
_facts: Pub on this site since 1820; John Mulligan took the lease in 1854
James Joyce mentions it in 'Counterparts' from Dubliners
Theatre Royal was nearby; showbills and photos from it still hang on the walls
Judy Garland performed at the Theatre Royal and drank here after the show
Long associated with Dublin's journalists and writers

s: Where Bloom had his gorgonzola sandwich — and Collins held cabinet meetings upstairs
w: Open since 1889 and immortalised in Ulysses ch. 8: Bloom stops here just before 2 pm for gorgonzola and burgundy. Michael Collins and Arthur Griffith held cabinet meetings upstairs in the early 1900s. Padraic O Conaire would tether his donkey to the lamp post outside while he drank.
_facts: Opened 1889 by Davy Byrne from County Wicklow
Featured in Ulysses ch. 8: Bloom has gorgonzola sandwich and glass of burgundy
Michael Collins and Arthur Griffith held cabinet meetings upstairs in the early 1900s
Regular haunt of Flann O'Brien, Brendan Behan, and Padraic O Conaire
O Conaire reportedly tethered his donkey to the lamp post outside while drinking here

s: Sessions every day — saved from a hotel developer by thousands of Dubliners in the street
w: The Mulligan family has run this North King Street pub since 1987, filling it with multiple traditional sessions daily. In 2021 a developer proposed a nine-storey hotel around its back; thousands protested and Dublin City Council refused planning permission. One of the few city-centre pubs where the music is the reason.
_facts: Run by the Mulligan family since 1987
Hosts multiple traditional Irish music sessions daily
2021: developer proposed nine-storey hotel wrapped around the pub
Dublin City Council refused planning permission after mass public protests
Considered one of the last authentic session pubs in central Dublin

s: Working presses and 10,000 artefacts of the trade — inside a Victorian barracks chapel
w: Housed in the garrison chapel of Beggars Bush Barracks, the Print Museum opened in 1996 with working presses, type-cases, and 10,000 items spanning centuries of the craft. Volunteers still set type by hand and run the machines for visitors. The barracks itself has its own thick history — and the chapel is unexpectedly beautiful.
_facts: Located in the Victorian garrison chapel of Beggars Bush Barracks, Dublin
Opened 1996; holds around 10,000 printing artefacts
Working printing equipment: Linotype machines, letterpress presses, bindery tools
Volunteers demonstrate working presses for visitors
Beggars Bush Barracks was the first barracks handed to the Irish Free State in 1922

s: Run round it three times at midnight and the devil appears — or so they say
w: John Semple built this 1830 Church of Ireland chapel from calp limestone — black when wet, hence the name. The interior has no walls; the exterior arches sweep straight to the ceiling in a parabolic vault. Local lore says running round it three times at midnight summons the devil. Deconsecrated 1962, now offices.
_facts: Built 1830 by architect John Semple, Church of Ireland
Calp limestone turns dark when wet — origin of the 'Black Church' name
Unique interior: no inner walls; exterior walls arch directly to form a parabolic vault
Local legend: circling it three times at midnight summons the devil
Deconsecrated 1962; converted to offices

s: Dublin's secret garden, surrounded by buildings — saved because UCD moved to Belfield
w: Entirely surrounded by Georgian buildings, almost impossible to find from the street. Laid out in 1865 for a permanent arts exhibition; the Guinness family held them privately until 1939, when Lord Iveagh gave them to the Irish state on the condition they 'remain unbuilt on'. In 1961 UCD considered building here — then moved to Belfield instead.
_facts: Laid out 1865 for the Dublin Exhibition Palace and Winter Garden
Surrounded by buildings on all sides, difficult to locate
Acquired by the Guinness family in 1870; gifted to the Irish state by Lord Iveagh in 1939
Gift required that the gardens 'remain unbuilt on' as a city lung
In 1961 UCD considered building here; the university instead relocated to Belfield

s: Gandon's masterpiece — burned by the IRA in 1921, rebuilt with noticeably darker stone
w: James Gandon built this neoclassical pile 1781-1791 on a swamp, despite a mob that repeatedly disrupted the foundations at the direction of the High Sheriff. In May 1921 the IRA burned it to destroy British tax records and the dome collapsed. Rebuilt in darker Ardbraccan limestone to showcase Irish materials. You can still see the join.
_facts: Designed by James Gandon, built 1781-1791 on reclaimed swampland
Construction disrupted by the High Sheriff with mobs of several thousand, backed by Dublin Corporation
Cost £200,000 to build
IRA burned the building in May 1921 to destroy British tax records; dome collapsed
Rebuilt in darker Irish Ardbraccan limestone — the colour difference is still visible today

s: Art Deco cafe with Harry Clarke windows — on the site of Wellington's old school
w: The Bewley family broke the East India Company's tea monopoly in 1835 by shipping direct from Canton. Their Grafton Street flagship opened in 1927 on the site of Whyte's Academy, where Wellington and Robert Emmet were educated. The Art Deco interior has stained-glass windows by Harry Clarke. Beckett drank coffee here.
_facts: Bewley's Quakers imported 2,099 chests of tea directly from Canton in 1835, breaking the East India Company monopoly
Grafton Street cafe opened 1927 by Ernest Bewley
Site of Whyte's Academy, whose pupils included the Duke of Wellington and Robert Emmet
Interior has stained-glass windows by Harry Clarke
Art Deco facade with rare Egyptian Revival mosaic

s: Looks like one room from outside — actually has 16 rooms across three floors
w: William Chambers designed this 1758 neoclassical folly for Lord Charlemont after his Grand Tour. It appears to be a single room behind four columns. Inside: 16 rooms across three floors. Four hollow columns drain rainwater via chains. The rooftop funerary urns are chimneys. Its tunnel served as a shooting range for Michael Collins in the 1920s.
_facts: Designed by William Chambers, built 1758-1775 for Lord Charlemont
Exterior gives the illusion of a single-roomed structure; interior has 16 rooms over three floors
Four hollow columns drain rainwater via internal chains
Rooftop funerary urns are functional chimneys
Underground tunnel used as a shooting range by Michael Collins in the 1920s

s: Mummified remains in the vaults — and the organ Handel may have played for the Messiah
w: First chapel here from 1095 — the only surviving Viking-foundation parish north of the Liffey. The limestone vaults have kept the air so dry that bodies have mummified for 400 years: a supposed crusader over six feet tall, a 400-year-old nun, and the Sheares brothers of 1798. The 1724 organ is said to be the one Handel used composing the Messiah.
_facts: First chapel on this site from 1095 — only surviving Viking-foundation parish north of the Liffey
Current structure 1686, designed by William Robinson
Limestone vaults preserve mummified remains including a 'crusader', a 400-year-old nun, and the Sheares brothers
Sheares brothers executed in 1798 for their role in the rebellion
1724 organ said to be the one on which Handel composed the Messiah

s: Red cylinder at the tip of what was once the world's longest sea wall
w: First lit in 1767 by candlepower, rebuilt in its current form in 1820. The red lighthouse stands at the end of the Great South Wall — at the time of its construction, the longest sea wall in the world. The four-mile walk from Ringsend delivers a 360-degree panorama: Howth, the Wicklow mountains, the Poolbeg chimneys.
_facts: First established 1767 running on candlepower; rebuilt in current cylindrical form in 1820
Stands on the Great South Wall, which was the world's longest sea wall at time of building
The wall extends nearly four miles from Ringsend into Dublin Bay
Painted red; accessible by a four-mile walk along the wall
Panoramic views of Dublin Bay, Howth, the Wicklow mountains

s: A 140-year-old Victorian interior — no music, no screens, and the clocks disagree
w: Opened 1881 and barely changed since: an extraordinary mahogany bar running the full depth of the room, original mirrors and chandeliers intact. No music, no screens. The clocks behind the bar all show different times, and the staff make a point of it. One of the most complete Victorian pub interiors still standing in Dublin.
_facts: South Great George's Street pub dating from 1881
Named for its extraordinary depth — one of the longest bars in Dublin
Original Victorian interior: mahogany bar, mirrors, brass fittings, chandeliers
No-music policy; multiple clocks behind the bar showing different times
Considered one of the best-preserved Victorian pub interiors in the city

s: Pearse's school, run through Irish — he left for the GPO in 1916 and was executed
w: Patrick Pearse opened St Enda's here in 1910, teaching entirely in Irish at a time when that was a cultural provocation. He left for the GPO in April 1916 and was executed six weeks later. His sister Margaret kept the school alive until 1935. The house and 50-acre park are barely changed. His classroom, his desk, the garden where he taught outdoors.
_facts: St Enda's School opened here 1910 by Patrick Pearse, teaching entirely through Irish
Pearse left in April 1916 for the Easter Rising and was executed
Sister Margaret Pearse kept the school running until 1935
Now a museum in a 50-acre park in Rathfarnham
House and grounds largely unchanged from the early 20th century

s: Ireland's first classical building — on a Knights Hospitaller site founded by Strongbow in 1174
w: Commissioned by the Duke of Ormond in 1679 on land that housed a Knights Hospitaller priory since Strongbow's 1174 grant — Ireland's first large classical building. Retired Irish Army soldiers lived here until 1927. After decades of dereliction, it was restored in 1985 and now houses IMMA in one of the country's finest 17th-century courtyards.
_facts: Land was a Knights Hospitaller priory from 1174, founded by Strongbow
Built 1679-1687, designed by Sir William Robinson for the Duke of Ormond
Ireland's first large-scale classical building
Functioned as retirement home for Irish Army soldiers until 1927
Restored by 1985; now houses the Irish Museum of Modern Art (IMMA)

s: Where scientists first identified the potato blight that caused the Great Famine
w: Founded in 1795 by the Royal Dublin Society on a 16-acre Glasnevin estate. In 1845, staff here were the first in Ireland to identify Phytophthora infestans — the infection destroying the potato crop — while the Famine was still unfolding. Nineteen hectares of living plant collections now adjoin Glasnevin Cemetery on one side and the River Tolka on the other.
_facts: Founded 1795 by the Royal Dublin Society
Staff first identified the Great Famine potato blight (Phytophthora infestans) in Ireland in 1845
Contains approximately 20,000 living plant species on 19.5 hectares
Curvilinear glasshouse range designed by Richard Turner
Situated between Glasnevin Cemetery and the River Tolka

s: The Irish constitution was drafted in Room 112 in 1922 — now called the Constitution Room
w: Martin Burke opened this hotel in 1824 in three Stephen's Green townhouses. During Easter 1916, British troops fired from its windows on the Irish Citizen Army in the Green below. In 1922, the Constitution of the Irish Free State was drafted in Room 112 — still shown to visitors as The Constitution Room.
_facts: Founded 1824 by Martin Burke from County Tipperary
1867 facade features four cast-iron torcheres from the Val d'Osne foundry, Paris
During Easter Rising 1916, British troops occupied the hotel and fired across St Stephen's Green
Constitution of the Irish Free State drafted in Room 112 in 1922 ('The Constitution Room')
William Makepeace Thackeray stayed here in 1842 and wrote about it
